1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
153

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.

6	import Mathlib
7	import AlgebraicJacobian.Cohomology.StructureSheafModuleK
8	import AlgebraicJacobian.RiemannRoch.WeilDivisor
9	
10	/-!
11	# Vanishing of `H¹` for skyscraper sheaves on a curve (RR.2.H¹)
12	
13	This file is the **RR.2.H¹** project-side build of the closed-point
14	skyscraper `H¹`-vanishing identity
15	
16	  `dim_{k̄} H¹(C, skyscraperSheaf P (ModuleCat.of k̄ k̄)) = 0`
17	
18	on a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / k̄`. The file is
19	the iter-191 Lane H **file-skeleton**: each of the eight pinned
20	declarations carries the *intended* substantive type signature (matching
21	the blueprint `\lean{...}` pin in `chapters/RiemannRoch_H1Vanishing.tex`),
22	with `sorry` bodies; the iter-192+ closure follows the classical
23	Hartshorne III.2.5 flasque-vanishing argument.
24	
25	The chapter strategy (Hartshorne III §2): an injective resolution of a
26	flasque sheaf yields a flasque quotient at each stage; the
27	global-sections functor is exact on a flasque-to-flasque-to-flasque
28	short exact sequence; hence the right-derived global-sections functor
29	vanishes on a flasque input in positive degree. We specialise the
30	abstract flasque-vanishing to the closed-point skyscraper sheaf, which
31	is flasque because it is the pushforward, along the closed embedding of
32	a one-point subspace, of the constant sheaf on an irreducible base.
33	
34	## Eight pinned declarations
35	
36	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque` — predicate on
37	   `Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)`.
38	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.pushforward` — pushforward of a
39	   flasque sheaf is flasque (Hartshorne II.1, Ex. 1.16(d)).
40	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.constant_of_irreducible` —
41	   constant sheaf on an irreducible space is flasque (Hartshorne
42	   II.1, Ex. 1.16(a)).
43	4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule_flasque_eq_zero` — flasque sheaves
44	   have zero `HModule` in positive degree (Hartshorne III.2.5).
45	5. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward_const` —
46	   skyscraper sheaf is the pushforward of a constant sheaf along the
47	   closed embedding of the closure of the support point.
48	6. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.closure_isIrreducible` — the
49	   closure of the support point of a `PrimeDivisor` is irreducible.
50	7. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque` —
51	   closed-point skyscraper sheaf is flasque.
52	8. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero` — the
53	   `RR.2.H¹` headline (`dim_{k̄} H¹(C, k(P)) = 0`), obtained by composing
54	   declarations 4 and 7.
55	
56	The eighth declaration also lives as a `private` typed-`sorry` helper at
57	`AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean`. The `private` modifier
58	mangles the internal name of the RRFormula copy, so the public
59	declaration in this file is the one resolved by the blueprint's

80	/-! ## §1. Flasque sheaves of `kbar`-modules -/
81	
82	/-- **Flasque sheaf of `kbar`-modules on a topological space**
83	(Hartshorne II.1, Exercise 1.16; III.2, paragraph preceding Lemma 2.4).
84	
85	A sheaf `F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)`
86	is **flasque** when, for every inclusion of opens `V ≤ U` in `X`, the
87	restriction map
88	`F.val.obj (op U) → F.val.obj (op V)` is surjective as a map of
89	`kbar`-modules.
90	
91	The predicate is read off the underlying presheaf of `kbar`-modules and
92	applies uniformly to the project's `ModuleCat kbar`-flavoured cohomology
93	pipeline (it does not depend on any topological-space hypothesis on `X`,
94	so the curve specialisation is by instance synthesis on
95	`C.left.toTopCat`).
96	
97	Blueprint reference: `def:isFlasque_sheaf`. -/
98	def Scheme.IsFlasque
99	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}}
100	    (F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)) : Prop :=
101	  ∀ ⦃U V : TopologicalSpace.Opens X⦄ (h : V ≤ U),
102	    Function.Surjective ((F.val.map (homOfLE h).op).hom)
103	
104	/-- **Pushforward of a flasque sheaf is flasque** (Hartshorne II.1,
105	Exercise 1.16(d)).
106	
107	For a continuous map of topological spaces `f : X ⟶ Y` and a flasque
108	sheaf `F` of `kbar`-modules on `X`, the pushforward sheaf `f _* F` is
109	flasque on `Y`. The reason is purely formal: the restriction map of
110	`f _* F` along `V ≤ U` in `Y` is, by definition of pushforward, the
111	restriction map of `F` along `f ⁻¹ V ≤ f ⁻¹ U` in `X`, which is
112	surjective by hypothesis on `F`.
113	
114	**iter-191 Lane H prover dispatch** — closed via the unfolding
115	`pushforward.map (homOfLE h).op = F.val.map ((Opens.map f).map (homOfLE h)).op`
116	(by `rfl`), so flasqueness of `F` along `(Opens.map f).map (homOfLE h)` gives
117	the conclusion. Blueprint reference: `lem:isFlasque_pushforward`. -/
118	theorem Scheme.IsFlasque.pushforward
119	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X Y : TopCat.{u}} (f : X ⟶ Y)
120	    {F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)}
121	    (hF : Scheme.IsFlasque F) :
122	    Scheme.IsFlasque
123	      ((TopCat.Sheaf.pushforward (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) f).obj F) := by
124	  intro U V h
125	  exact hF ((Opens.map f).map (homOfLE h)).le
126	
127	/-- **Constant sheaf on an irreducible topological space is flasque**
128	(Hartshorne II.1, Exercise 1.16(a)).
129	
130	For an irreducible topological space `X`, every nonempty open is
131	connected and dense; the constant presheaf `U ↦ A` already satisfies the
132	sheaf condition, and its restriction maps are the identity on `A` (for
133	the nonempty branches) and factor through `0` (for the empty branch).
134	
135	**iter-191 Lane H file-skeleton** — Tier-3 honest typed sorry; closure
136	is iter-192+. Blueprint reference:
137	`lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible`. -/
138	theorem Scheme.IsFlasque.constant_of_irreducible
139	    (kbar : Type u) [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}}
140	    [IrreducibleSpace X] (A : ModuleCat.{u} kbar) :
141	    Scheme.IsFlasque
142	      ((constantSheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
143	        (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)).obj A) := by
144	  sorry

146	/-- **Injective sheaves have vanishing higher cohomology** (axiom-clean
147	helper, Hartshorne III §1).
148	
149	For a topological space `X` and an injective object `I` of the
150	`kbar`-module sheaf category, the `kbar`-flavoured derived
151	global-sections cohomology `HModule kbar I i` is the zero `kbar`-module
152	for every `i ≥ 1`. This is immediate from Mathlib's
153	`HasInjectiveDimensionLT` framework: `Injective I` gives
154	`HasInjectiveDimensionLT I 1` (Mathlib
155	`instHasInjectiveDimensionLTOfNatNatOfInjective`), and
156	`HasInjectiveDimensionLT.subsingleton` yields
157	`Subsingleton (Abelian.Ext Y I i)` for `i ≥ 1` and any `Y`; specialising
158	`Y` to the constant sheaf at `kbar` and applying
159	`Module.finrank_zero_of_subsingleton` closes the goal.
160	
161	**iter-192 Lane H prover dispatch** — closed axiom-clean. Used as the
162	`Ext^i(_, I) = 0` input inside the Hartshorne III.2.5 long-exact-sequence
163	chase for `HModule_flasque_eq_zero` below. -/
164	theorem Scheme.HModule_injective_finrank_eq_zero
165	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}}
166	    [HasSheafify (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)]
167	    [HasExt (Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar))]
168	    {I : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)}
169	    [Injective I] (i : ℕ) (hi : 1 ≤ i) :
170	    Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar I i) = 0 := by
171	  have hsub : Subsingleton (Scheme.HModule kbar I i) :=
172	    HasInjectiveDimensionLT.subsingleton I 1 i hi _
173	  exact Module.finrank_zero_of_subsingleton
174	
175	/-- **The canonical injective-embedding short exact sequence**
176	`0 → F → Injective.under F → cokernel(Injective.ι F) → 0` of sheaves of
177	`kbar`-modules (axiom-clean helper). The embedding `Injective.ι F` is
178	mono by `Injective.ι_mono`, the cokernel projection is epi by
179	construction, and the middle row is exact at `Injective.under F` by
180	`ShortComplex.exact_cokernel`. -/
181	noncomputable def Scheme.injectiveSES
182	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}}
183	    [HasSheafify (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)]
184	    [HasExt (Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar))]
185	    (F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)) :
186	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex
187	      (Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)) :=
188	  ShortComplex.mk (Injective.ι F) (Limits.cokernel.π (Injective.ι F))
189	    (Limits.cokernel.condition (Injective.ι F))
190	
191	/-- The injective-embedding short complex is short exact. -/
192	theorem Scheme.injectiveSES_shortExact
193	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}}
194	    [HasSheafify (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)]
195	    [HasExt (Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar))]
196	    (F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)) :
197	    (Scheme.injectiveSES F).ShortExact :=
198	  { exact := ShortComplex.exact_cokernel (Injective.ι F)
199	    mono_f := Injective.ι_mono F
200	    epi_g := Limits.coequalizer.π_epi }
201	
202	/-- **Generic LES vanishing lemma**: in an abelian category with enough Ext,
203	if `0 → S.X₁ → S.X₂ → S.X₃ → 0` is short exact with `S.X₂` injective and the
204	post-composition `Hom(X, S.X₂) → Hom(X, S.X₃)` is surjective, then
205	`Ext X S.X₁ 1` is the zero `Subsingleton`.
206	
207	This is the structural skeleton of the Hartshorne III.2.5 argument
208	specialised at degree 1: the long exact sequence
209	`Ext X S.X₂ 0 → Ext X S.X₃ 0 → Ext X S.X₁ 1 → Ext X S.X₂ 1`
210	collapses because the rightmost term vanishes (injectivity of `S.X₂`)
211	and the leftmost map is surjective by the `Hom`-level hypothesis. The
212	proof composes:
213	- `HasInjectiveDimensionLT.subsingleton` (injective ⇒ `Ext X S.X₂ 1 = 0`).
214	- `Abelian.Ext.covariant_sequence_exact₁` (any `x₁ ∈ Ext X S.X₁ 1` lifts
215	  to some `x₃ ∈ Ext X S.X₃ 0` via the connecting morphism).
216	- `Abelian.Ext.addEquiv₀` (identifies `Ext X S.X₃ 0` with `Hom(X, S.X₃)`).
217	- `comp_extClass_assoc` (the LES "complex" identity used to discharge
218	  `(Ext.mk₀ S.g).comp extClass = 0`).
219	
220	**iter-192 Lane H prover dispatch** — closed axiom-clean. Used to peel
221	off the `i = 1` case of `HModule_flasque_eq_zero` once the
222	`Hom(X, I) → Hom(X, G)`-surjectivity input (Hartshorne II Ex. 1.16(b))
223	is supplied for an injective resolution. -/
224	theorem ext_one_eq_zero_of_hom_surjective_of_injective
225	    {C : Type v} [Category.{u} C] [Abelian C] [HasExt C]
226	    (X : C) {S : ShortComplex C} (hS : S.ShortExact) [Injective S.X₂]
227	    (hsurj : Function.Surjective (fun (f : X ⟶ S.X₂) => f ≫ S.g))
228	    (x₁ : Abelian.Ext X S.X₁ 1) : x₁ = 0 := by
229	  have hinj_subs : Subsingleton (Abelian.Ext X S.X₂ 1) :=
230	    HasInjectiveDimensionLT.subsingleton S.X₂ 1 1 le_rfl _
231	  have hker : x₁.comp (Abelian.Ext.mk₀ S.f) (show (1 : ℕ) + 0 = 1 by omega) = 0 :=
232	    Subsingleton.elim _ _
233	  obtain ⟨x₃, hx₃⟩ :=
234	    Abelian.Ext.covariant_sequence_exact₁ X hS x₁ hker
235	      (n₀ := 0) (show 0 + 1 = 1 by omega)
236	  obtain ⟨y, hy⟩ := hsurj (Abelian.Ext.addEquiv₀ x₃)
237	  simp only at hy
238	  have hx3eq : Abelian.Ext.mk₀ (Abelian.Ext.addEquiv₀ x₃) = x₃ := by
239	    rw [← Abelian.Ext.addEquiv₀_symm_apply, AddEquiv.symm_apply_apply]
240	  have hx3_factored :
241	      x₃ = (Abelian.Ext.mk₀ y).comp (Abelian.Ext.mk₀ S.g)
242	        (show (0 : ℕ) + 0 = 0 by omega) := by
243	    rw [Abelian.Ext.mk₀_comp_mk₀, hy, hx3eq]
244	  rw [← hx₃, hx3_factored]
245	  rw [Abelian.Ext.comp_assoc (Abelian.Ext.mk₀ y) (Abelian.Ext.mk₀ S.g) hS.extClass
246	      (show (0 : ℕ) + 0 = 0 by omega) (show (0 : ℕ) + 1 = 1 by omega)
247	      (show (0 : ℕ) + 0 + 1 = 1 by omega)]
248	  have hSg_extClass_zero :
249	      (Abelian.Ext.mk₀ S.g).comp hS.extClass (show (0 : ℕ) + 1 = 1 by omega) = 0 := by
250	    have key := hS.comp_extClass_assoc (Y := S.X₁) (n := 0)
251	      (Abelian.Ext.mk₀ (𝟙 S.X₁)) (n' := 1) (h := show (1 : ℕ) + 0 = 1 by omega)
252	    rw [Abelian.Ext.comp_mk₀_id] at key
253	    exact key
254	  rw [hSg_extClass_zero, Abelian.Ext.comp_zero]
255	
256	/-- **Higher-degree LES vanishing lemma** (axiom-clean structural helper,
257	iter-193 Lane H prover dispatch).
258	
259	Given a short exact sequence `0 → S.X₁ → S.X₂ → S.X₃ → 0` in an abelian
260	category with injective `S.X₂` and `n₀ ≥ 1`, if every element of
261	`Abelian.Ext X S.X₃ n₀` is zero, then so is every element of
262	`Abelian.Ext X S.X₁ (n₀ + 1)`. This is the higher-degree analogue of
263	`ext_one_eq_zero_of_hom_surjective_of_injective`: at degrees `n₀ ≥ 1` the
264	`Hom`-surjectivity hypothesis is replaced by the (stronger, recursable)
265	"`Ext^{n₀}(_, S.X₃) = 0`" hypothesis.
266	
267	**Proof structure**: by `HasInjectiveDimensionLT.subsingleton` applied to
268	`S.X₂` injective at degree `n₀ + 1 ≥ 2`, `Abelian.Ext X S.X₂ (n₀ + 1)` is
269	subsingleton, so the LES bracket `Ext X S.X₃ n₀ →ᵟ Ext X S.X₁ (n₀+1) →
270	Ext X S.X₂ (n₀+1)` makes the connecting morphism δ surjective.
271	Combined with the source `Ext X S.X₃ n₀ = 0`, the conclusion follows.
272	Used in the `i ≥ 2` case of `Scheme.HModule_flasque_eq_zero`. -/
273	theorem ext_succ_eq_zero_of_injective_of_lower_zero
274	    {C : Type v} [Category.{u} C] [Abelian C] [HasExt C]
275	    (X : C) {S : ShortComplex C} (hS : S.ShortExact) [Injective S.X₂]
276	    {n₀ : ℕ} (h_n₀ : 1 ≤ n₀)
277	    (hX₃ : ∀ y : Abelian.Ext X S.X₃ n₀, y = 0)
278	    (x₁ : Abelian.Ext X S.X₁ (n₀ + 1)) : x₁ = 0 := by
279	  have hinj_subs : Subsingleton (Abelian.Ext X S.X₂ (n₀ + 1)) :=
280	    HasInjectiveDimensionLT.subsingleton S.X₂ 1 (n₀ + 1) (by omega) _
281	  have hker : x₁.comp (Abelian.Ext.mk₀ S.f)
282	      (show (n₀ + 1) + 0 = (n₀ + 1) by omega) = 0 :=
283	    Subsingleton.elim _ _
284	  obtain ⟨x₃, hx₃⟩ :=
285	    Abelian.Ext.covariant_sequence_exact₁ X hS x₁ hker (rfl : n₀ + 1 = n₀ + 1)
286	  rw [hX₃ x₃] at hx₃
287	  rw [← hx₃, Abelian.Ext.zero_comp]
288	
289	/-- **Hartshorne II.1, Exercise 1.16(b)** (sections form): the
290	sheaf-morphism `S.g` is sectionwise-surjective on a short exact sequence
291	whose leftmost sheaf is flasque.
292	
293	For a sheaf-level short exact sequence
294	`0 → S.X₁ → S.X₂ → S.X₃ → 0` in `Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
295	(ModuleCat kbar)` with `S.X₁` flasque, the section-level map
296	`(S.g.val.app (op U)).hom : S.X₂.val.obj (op U) → S.X₃.val.obj (op U)`
297	is surjective for every open `U`.
298	
299	**Proof sketch** (Hartshorne II.1 Ex 1.16(b), Zorn's lemma):
300	Let `t ∈ S.X₃.val.obj (op U)`. Consider pairs `(V, s)` where `V ⊆ U` is
301	open and `s ∈ S.X₂.val.obj (op V)` restricts to `t|_V`. Order by
302	extension. Any chain has an upper bound (sheaf gluing condition on
303	`S.X₂`). By Zorn's lemma, a maximal element `(V₀, s₀)` exists. Suppose
304	`V₀ ≠ U`. Pick `x ∈ U \ V₀`. Stalkwise, `S.X₂_x → S.X₃_x` is surjective
305	(SES in the abelian sheaf category preserves stalks). So in some
306	neighborhood `W` of `x` (after shrinking), there is
307	`s'_W ∈ S.X₂.val.obj (op W)` with image `t|_W`. On `V₀ ∩ W`,
308	`s₀ - s'_W` lies in `S.X₁.val.obj (op (V₀ ∩ W))` (since both map to
309	`t|_{V₀∩W}`). Use flasqueness of `S.X₁` to extend the difference to
310	`α ∈ S.X₁.val.obj (op W)`. Set `s'' := s'_W + α`. Then `s''` agrees with
311	`s₀` on `V₀ ∩ W`. By sheaf gluing on `S.X₂`, get a unique
312	`s''' ∈ S.X₂.val.obj (op (V₀ ∪ W))` extending both. This contradicts
313	maximality. Hence `V₀ = U`.
314	
315	**Tier-3 typed sorry** — the proof requires Zorn's lemma + stalkwise
316	SES surjection + sheaf gluing + flasqueness extension; estimate
317	~150-200 LOC, scheduled iter-194+. Blueprint reference: out-of-scope
318	subsection of `thm:H1_vanishing_flasque` (the Hartshorne II.1 Ex 1.16(b)
319	input). -/

323	    {S : CategoryTheory.ShortComplex
324	      (Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar))}
325	    (hS : S.ShortExact)
326	    (hF : Scheme.IsFlasque S.X₁)
327	    (U : TopologicalSpace.Opens X) :
328	    Function.Surjective ((S.g.hom.app (Opposite.op U)).hom) := by
329	  sorry

331	/-- **Hartshorne II.1, Exercise 1.16(c)** (project-side cokernel
332	inheritance, axiom-clean):
333	
334	The cokernel of a flasque-by-flasque short exact sequence is flasque.
335	
336	For a sheaf-level short exact sequence
337	`0 → S.X₁ → S.X₂ → S.X₃ → 0` in `Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
338	(ModuleCat kbar)` with both `S.X₁` and `S.X₂` flasque, `S.X₃` is also
339	flasque. The hypothesis `h_b` packages the Hartshorne II.1 Ex 1.16(b)
340	sections-surjectivity input as a parameter (rather than calling
341	`Scheme.IsFlasque.shortExact_app_surjective` directly), keeping this
342	lemma's axiom-set clean — `sorryAx` traces only through the consumer
343	site, not through this declaration.
344	
345	**Proof**: for `V ≤ U`, given `t ∈ S.X₃.val.obj (op V)`, lift via `h_b` at
346	`V` to `t̃ ∈ S.X₂.val.obj (op V)`, extend via flasqueness of `S.X₂` from
347	`V` to `U` getting `T̃ ∈ S.X₂.val.obj (op U)`, then set
348	`T := S.g.val.app (op U) T̃ ∈ S.X₃.val.obj (op U)`. The restriction of
349	`T` to `V` equals `t` by naturality of `S.g.val` and the lift property of

353	`i ≥ 2` case of `HModule_flasque_eq_zero` to inherit flasqueness on the

356	theorem Scheme.IsFlasque.cokernel_of_shortExact_flasque_flasque
357	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}}
358	    [HasSheafify (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)]
359	    {S : CategoryTheory.ShortComplex
360	      (Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar))}
361	    (hI : Scheme.IsFlasque S.X₂)
362	    (h_b : ∀ (U : TopologicalSpace.Opens X),
363	      Function.Surjective ((S.g.hom.app (Opposite.op U)).hom)) :
364	    Scheme.IsFlasque S.X₃ := by
365	  intro U V hVU t

368	  refine ⟨(S.g.hom.app (Opposite.op U)).hom TExt, ?_⟩

371	  --       (S.X₃.val.map _).hom ((S.g.hom.app (op U)).hom TExt)

380	`V ⊆ U` open, the open immersion `j_V : V ↪ X` gives `j_{V!}(O_V)
381	↪ j_{U!}(O_U)`, and `Hom(j_{U!}(O_U), I) → Hom(j_{V!}(O_V), I)` is
382	surjective by injectivity of `I`. Translating via the
383	`j_{(-)!}` ⊣ `j_{(-)}*` adjunction (or the equivalent presheaf form for
384	the constant ring sheaf `kbar`), this gives surjectivity of `I(U) → I(V)`.

398	/-- **Auxiliary subsingleton lemma for `HModule_flasque_eq_zero`**
399	(iter-193 Lane H prover dispatch).
400	
401	For a flasque sheaf `F` of `kbar`-modules on `X` and any `n : ℕ`, the
402	`(n+1)`-th derived global-sections cohomology `HModule kbar F (n+1)` is
403	subsingleton (i.e., has at most one element). This statement is the
404	strong-induction-on-`n` carrier of the main theorem
405	`HModule_flasque_eq_zero`: the `F`-generalised quantifier inside the
406	`induction` block lets the inductive step apply the IH on the flasque
407	quotient sheaf at one lower degree. Once subsingleton is established,
408	`Module.finrank_zero_of_subsingleton` gives the `finrank = 0` conclusion
409	in the main statement. -/
410	private theorem Scheme.HModule_flasque_subsingleton_aux
411	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}}
412	    [HasSheafify (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)]
413	    [HasExt (Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar))]
414	    (n : ℕ) :
415	    ∀ {F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)},
416	      Scheme.IsFlasque F → Subsingleton (Scheme.HModule kbar F (n + 1)) := by
417	  induction n with
418	  | zero =>
419	    intro F hF
420	    refine ⟨fun x y => ?_⟩
421	    -- i = 1 case: use ext_one_eq_zero_of_hom_surjective_of_injective on the
422	    -- canonical injective SES `0 → F → Injective.under F → cokernel → 0`.
423	    have hSES := Scheme.injectiveSES_shortExact F
424	    -- Injective.under F is injective by Mathlib's instance.
425	    have hI_inj : Injective (Scheme.injectiveSES F).X₂ := Injective.injective_under F
426	    -- 1.16(b) at U = ⊤ gives section-level surjectivity, which we lift to
427	    -- Hom-from-(constantSheaf k)-surjectivity via the explicit `constantSheaf
428	    -- ⊣ (sheafSections at ⊤)` adjunction (`constantSheafAdj` with terminal
429	    -- `⊤ ∈ Opens X` via `Preorder.isTerminalTop`) and the rank-1 free
430	    -- structure of `ModuleCat.of kbar kbar` (a kbar-linear hom from `kbar`
431	    -- is determined by its image of `1`, with the inverse map
432	    -- `LinearMap.toSpanSingleton kbar _ s` for any chosen lift `s`).
433	    have hsurj : Function.Surjective
434	        (fun (f : (constantSheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
435	            (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)).obj (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar) ⟶
436	            (Scheme.injectiveSES F).X₂) => f ≫ (Scheme.injectiveSES F).g) := by
437	      intro g
438	      -- Set up the adjunction at the terminal `⊤ ∈ Opens X`.
439	      let hT : Limits.IsTerminal (⊤ : TopologicalSpace.Opens X) :=
440	        Preorder.isTerminalTop (TopologicalSpace.Opens X)
441	      let adj := constantSheafAdj (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
442	        (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) hT
443	      -- 1.16(b) at U = ⊤ gives section-level surjectivity.
444	      have h_b_top : Function.Surjective
445	          (((Scheme.injectiveSES F).g.hom.app
446	            (Opposite.op (⊤ : TopologicalSpace.Opens X))).hom) :=
447	        Scheme.IsFlasque.shortExact_app_surjective hSES hF ⊤
448	      -- Convert `g` to a section-level morphism via `adj.homEquiv`.
449	      let g_sec : ModuleCat.of kbar kbar ⟶
450	          (Scheme.injectiveSES F).X₃.val.obj
451	            (Opposite.op (⊤ : TopologicalSpace.Opens X)) :=
452	        adj.homEquiv _ _ g
453	      -- Pick the value of `g_sec` at `1 : kbar`.
454	      let s₃ : (Scheme.injectiveSES F).X₃.val.obj
455	          (Opposite.op (⊤ : TopologicalSpace.Opens X)) := g_sec.hom 1
456	      -- Apply 1.16(b) at ⊤ to get a lift in `X₂`.
457	      obtain ⟨s₂, hs₂⟩ := h_b_top s₃
458	      -- Construct the section-level lift `f_sec : kbar ⟶ X₂.val.obj (op ⊤)`.
459	      let f_sec : ModuleCat.of kbar kbar ⟶
460	          (Scheme.injectiveSES F).X₂.val.obj
461	            (Opposite.op (⊤ : TopologicalSpace.Opens X)) :=
462	        ModuleCat.ofHom (LinearMap.toSpanSingleton kbar _ s₂)
463	      -- Lift `f_sec` back to a sheaf-level morphism via `adj.homEquiv.symm`.
464	      refine ⟨(adj.homEquiv _ _).symm f_sec, ?_⟩
465	      -- Verify `(adj.homEquiv.symm f_sec) ≫ S.g = g` by applying `adj.homEquiv`.
466	      apply (adj.homEquiv _ _).injective
467	      rw [Adjunction.homEquiv_naturality_right, Equiv.apply_symm_apply]
468	      change f_sec ≫ ((sheafSections (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
469	        (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)).obj (Opposite.op ⊤)).map (Scheme.injectiveSES F).g = g_sec
470	      -- Verify the section-level equation at the linear map level.
471	      -- Two `kbar`-linear maps from `kbar` agree iff they agree at `1`.
472	      apply ModuleCat.hom_ext
473	      ext
474	      -- Goal (post `LinearMap.ext_ring`): both sides applied at `1`.
475	      change ((Scheme.injectiveSES F).g.hom.app (Opposite.op ⊤)).hom
476	          ((LinearMap.toSpanSingleton kbar _ s₂) 1) = g_sec.hom 1
477	      rw [LinearMap.toSpanSingleton_apply_one]
478	      exact hs₂
479	    rw [ext_one_eq_zero_of_hom_surjective_of_injective _ hSES hsurj x,
480	        ext_one_eq_zero_of_hom_surjective_of_injective _ hSES hsurj y]
481	  | succ m ih =>
482	    intro F hF
483	    refine ⟨fun x y => ?_⟩
484	    -- i = m + 2, m + 1 ≥ 1. Use the LES iso via the canonical injective SES,
485	    -- with the flasque quotient G inheriting flasqueness from F + Injective.under F
486	    -- (Hartshorne III Lemma 2.4 + II Ex 1.16(c)). Reduce to IH at degree m + 1.
487	    have hSES := Scheme.injectiveSES_shortExact F
488	    have hI_inj : Injective (Scheme.injectiveSES F).X₂ := Injective.injective_under F
489	    have hI_flasque : Scheme.IsFlasque (Scheme.injectiveSES F).X₂ :=
490	      Scheme.IsFlasque.injective_flasque _
491	    have h_b : ∀ U, Function.Surjective
492	        (((Scheme.injectiveSES F).g.hom.app (Opposite.op U)).hom) :=
493	      fun U => Scheme.IsFlasque.shortExact_app_surjective hSES hF U
494	    have hG_flasque : Scheme.IsFlasque (Scheme.injectiveSES F).X₃ :=
495	      Scheme.IsFlasque.cokernel_of_shortExact_flasque_flasque hI_flasque h_b
496	    have hG_sub : Subsingleton (Scheme.HModule kbar (Scheme.injectiveSES F).X₃ (m + 1)) :=
497	      ih hG_flasque
498	    have hX₃_zero :
499	        ∀ y : Abelian.Ext
500	            ((constantSheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
501	              (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)).obj (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar))
502	            (Scheme.injectiveSES F).X₃ (m + 1), y = 0 :=
503	      fun y => Subsingleton.elim (α := Scheme.HModule kbar (Scheme.injectiveSES F).X₃ (m + 1)) y 0
504	    have h_n₀ : 1 ≤ m + 1 := Nat.succ_le_succ (Nat.zero_le _)
505	    rw [ext_succ_eq_zero_of_injective_of_lower_zero _ hSES h_n₀ hX₃_zero x,
506	        ext_succ_eq_zero_of_injective_of_lower_zero _ hSES h_n₀ hX₃_zero y]
507	
508	/-- **Flasque sheaves have vanishing higher cohomology** (Hartshorne
509	III.2, Proposition 2.5).
510	
511	For a topological space `X` and a flasque sheaf `F` of `kbar`-modules on
512	`X`, the `kbar`-flavoured derived global-sections cohomology
513	`HModule kbar F i` is the zero `kbar`-module for every `i ≥ 1`. In
514	particular, `dim_{kbar} HModule kbar F 1 = 0`.
515	
516	The proof structure mirrors Hartshorne III §2 verbatim: embed `F` into
517	an injective `I` of the abelian sheaf category, form the quotient short
518	exact sequence `0 → F → I → G → 0` (Mathlib: `Scheme.injectiveSES`,
519	axiom-clean), observe that `G` inherits flasqueness from `F` and `I`
520	(the latter by Hartshorne III Lemma 2.4), read off the short exact
521	sequence on global sections from the flasque input
522	(Hartshorne II Ex. 1.16(b)), and apply the long exact sequence
523	(`Abelian.Ext.covariant_sequence_exact₁`) to get `HModule kbar F 1 = 0`
524	and a reduction `HModule kbar F i ≅ HModule kbar G (i - 1)` for `i ≥ 2`;
525	iteration closes the higher-degree cases.
526	
527	**iter-193 Lane H prover dispatch** — body fully closed structurally;
528	sorries are isolated to two named substrate helpers
529	(`shortExact_app_surjective` and `injective_flasque`, both Tier-3).
530	The proof decomposes cleanly into the two cases via the auxiliary
531	subsingleton lemma `Scheme.HModule_flasque_subsingleton_aux`:
532	* `i = 1`: invoke `ext_one_eq_zero_of_hom_surjective_of_injective` on the
533	  `injectiveSES F` short-exact data, supplying the Hartshorne II Ex 1.16(b)
534	  input. The Hom-from-constant-sheaf surjectivity is derived axiom-clean
535	  from `IsFlasque.shortExact_app_surjective` at `U = ⊤` via the explicit
536	  `constantSheafAdj` (at terminal `⊤ ∈ Opens X` via
537	  `Preorder.isTerminalTop`) + the rank-1 free structure of
538	  `ModuleCat.of kbar kbar` (`LinearMap.toSpanSingleton` lift).
539	* `i ≥ 2`: invoke `ext_succ_eq_zero_of_injective_of_lower_zero` on
540	  `injectiveSES F`, supplying `Subsingleton (HModule kbar G (i-1))` via the
541	  IH applied to the flasque quotient `G` (the latter via
542	  `IsFlasque.cokernel_of_shortExact_flasque_flasque` and
543	  `IsFlasque.injective_flasque`).
544	
545	Blueprint reference: `thm:H1_vanishing_flasque`. -/
546	theorem Scheme.HModule_flasque_eq_zero
547	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}}
548	    [HasSheafify (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)]
549	    [HasExt (Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar))]
550	    {F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)}
551	    (hF : Scheme.IsFlasque F) (i : ℕ) (hi : 1 ≤ i) :
552	    Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar F i) = 0 := by
553	  obtain ⟨n, rfl⟩ : ∃ n, n + 1 = i := ⟨i - 1, by omega⟩
554	  have hsub : Subsingleton (Scheme.HModule kbar F (n + 1)) :=
555	    Scheme.HModule_flasque_subsingleton_aux n hF
556	  exact Module.finrank_zero_of_subsingleton
557	
558	/-! ## §2. Skyscraper sheaves are flasque -/
559	
560	/-- **Skyscraper sheaf as pushforward of a constant sheaf** (Hartshorne
561	II.1, Exercise 1.17).
562	
563	The closed-point skyscraper sheaf `skyscraperSheaf P A` on a topological
564	space `X` is naturally isomorphic to the pushforward, along the constant
565	map `PUnit → X` at `P`, of the constant sheaf with value `A` on
566	`PUnit`. The constant sheaf on `PUnit` is itself isomorphic to the
567	skyscraper sheaf at `PUnit.unit`, so this is the sheaf-level
568	counterpart of Mathlib's presheaf-level
569	`skyscraperPresheaf_eq_pushforward` (Mathlib snapshot `b80f227`
570	`Topology/Sheaves/Skyscraper.lean`).
571	
572	For our usage on a curve, `PUnit` plays the role of `closure {P}`
573	(which on a Noetherian space with `P` closed is a singleton, hence
574	homeomorphic to `PUnit`).
575	
576	**iter-191 Lane H file-skeleton** — Tier-3 honest typed sorry; closure
577	is iter-192+. Blueprint reference:
578	`lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward`. -/
579	theorem Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward_const
580	    (kbar : Type u) [Field kbar]
581	    {X : TopCat.{u}} (P : X)
582	    [∀ U : TopologicalSpace.Opens X, Decidable (P ∈ U)]
583	    [∀ U : TopologicalSpace.Opens (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}),
584	      Decidable (PUnit.unit ∈ U)]
585	    (A : ModuleCat.{u} kbar) :
586	    Nonempty
587	      (skyscraperSheaf (C := ModuleCat.{u} kbar) P A ≅
588	        (TopCat.Sheaf.pushforward (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)
589	            (TopCat.ofHom (ContinuousMap.const (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}) P))).obj
590	          ((constantSheaf
591	              (Opens.grothendieckTopology (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}))
592	              (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)).obj A)) := by
593	  sorry
594	
595	/-- **Closure of the support point of a `PrimeDivisor` is irreducible**.
596	
597	For any scheme `X` and any `P : X.PrimeDivisor`, the topological
598	closure of the singleton `{P.point}` is an irreducible subset of `X`.
599	This is a project-bespoke ancillary that holds in full generality: the
600	closure of any irreducible set in a topological space is irreducible,
601	and a singleton `{x}` is irreducible (it is nonempty and contains no
602	proper non-empty closed subsets).
603	
604	**iter-191 Lane H prover dispatch** — closed via
605	`isIrreducible_singleton.closure`. Blueprint reference:
606	`lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible`. -/
607	theorem Scheme.PrimeDivisor.closure_isIrreducible
608	    {X : Scheme.{u}} (P : X.PrimeDivisor) :
609	    IsIrreducible (closure ({P.point} : Set X)) :=
610	  isIrreducible_singleton.closure
611	
612	/-- **The closed-point skyscraper sheaf is flasque**.
613	
614	For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / kbar` and a
615	prime divisor `P : C.left.PrimeDivisor` (equivalently, a closed point
616	on the curve), the closed-point skyscraper sheaf
617	`skyscraperSheaf P.point (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)` is flasque as a
618	sheaf of `kbar`-modules on the underlying topological space of `C`.
619	
620	The proof originally was planned to compose the four lemma-blocks of this
621	chapter (`skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward_const`,
622	`PrimeDivisor.closure_isIrreducible`, `IsFlasque.constant_of_irreducible`,
623	`IsFlasque.pushforward`). The iter-191 prover dispatch takes a more direct
624	route: unfold `skyscraperSheaf.val = skyscraperPresheaf`, then on the
625	`p ∈ V` branch the restriction map is an `eqToHom` (hence iso, hence
626	surjective in `ConcreteCategory`); on the `p ∉ V` branch the codomain is
627	the terminal object of `ModuleCat kbar`, which is the zero object, hence
628	`Subsingleton` on the underlying type. Either branch closes the
629	`Function.Surjective` obligation. This bypasses (3) and (5).
630	
631	**iter-191 Lane H prover dispatch** — closed directly via
632	`skyscraperPresheaf_map`. Blueprint reference:
633	`lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque`. -/
634	theorem Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque
635	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
636	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
637	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
638	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
639	    (P : C.left.PrimeDivisor)
640	    [∀ U : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left, Decidable (P.point ∈ U)] :
641	    Scheme.IsFlasque
642	      (skyscraperSheaf (C := ModuleCat.{u} kbar) P.point
643	        (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)) := by
644	  intro U V h
645	  change Function.Surjective
646	    (((skyscraperPresheaf P.point (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)).map
647	      (homOfLE h).op).hom)
648	  by_cases hV : P.point ∈ V
649	  · -- restriction is `eqToHom`, hence an iso, hence surjective
650	    have heq : (skyscraperPresheaf P.point (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)).obj
651	        (Opposite.op U) =
652	        (skyscraperPresheaf P.point (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)).obj
653	        (Opposite.op V) := by
654	      simp [skyscraperPresheaf, h hV, hV]
655	    have hmap := skyscraperPresheaf_map P.point (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)
656	      (i := (homOfLE h).op)
657	    rw [dif_pos hV] at hmap
658	    rw [hmap]
659	    have : IsIso (eqToHom heq) := inferInstance
660	    exact (ConcreteCategory.bijective_of_isIso (eqToHom heq)).2
661	  · -- codomain is zero (terminal in ModuleCat), so surjective trivially
662	    have hzero : Limits.IsZero
663	        ((skyscraperPresheaf P.point (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)).obj
664	          (Opposite.op V)) := by
665	      simp [skyscraperPresheaf, hV]
666	      exact (terminalIsTerminal).isZero
667	    have : Subsingleton ((skyscraperPresheaf P.point
668	        (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)).obj (Opposite.op V)) :=
669	      ModuleCat.subsingleton_of_isZero hzero
670	    intro y; exact ⟨0, Subsingleton.elim _ _⟩
671	
672	/-! ## §3. The closed-point skyscraper sheaf has vanishing `H¹` -/
673	
674	/-- **`H¹` of the closed-point skyscraper sheaf vanishes** (Hartshorne
675	III.2.5 applied to the flasque skyscraper).
676	
677	For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / kbar` and a
678	prime divisor `P : C.left.PrimeDivisor` (a closed point on the curve),
679	`dim_{kbar} H¹(C, skyscraperSheaf P (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)) = 0`.
680	
681	The proof composes the two substrate inputs of this chapter:
682	`skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque` (the skyscraper sheaf is flasque) and
683	`HModule_flasque_eq_zero` at `i = 1` (flasque ⇒ `H¹ = 0`); then
684	`Module.finrank kbar 0 = 0` closes the dimension identity.
685	
686	**iter-191 Lane H prover dispatch** — closed via composition of
687	`HModule_flasque_eq_zero` (still typed-`sorry` pending iter-192+
688	Hartshorne III.2.5 closure) and `skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque` (now closed
689	directly in this file) at `i = 1`. The same name also occurs as a
690	`private` typed-`sorry` helper at
691	`AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean`; the `private` modifier
692	mangles that copy's internal name, so the public name resolved by the
693	blueprint's `\lean{...}` pin (and by `sync_leanok`) is the declaration
694	below. Blueprint reference:
695	`lem:H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero_main`. -/
696	theorem Scheme.H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero
697	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
698	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
699	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
700	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
701	    (P : C.left.PrimeDivisor)
702	    [∀ U : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left, Decidable (P.point ∈ U)] :
703	    Module.finrank kbar
704	        (Scheme.HModule kbar
705	          (skyscraperSheaf (C := ModuleCat.{u} kbar) P.point
706	            (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)) 1) = 0 :=
707	  Scheme.HModule_flasque_eq_zero (Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque C P) 1 le_rfl
708	
709	end AlgebraicGeometry

2	
3	\chapter{Vanishing of \(H^1\) for skyscraper sheaves on a curve (RR.2.H\(^1\))}
4	\label{chap:RR_H1Vanishing}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.1, Exercise~1.16
7	% (flasque sheaves), Exercise~1.17 (skyscraper sheaves); III.2,
8	% Proposition~2.5 (flasque sheaves are acyclic) (read from
9	% references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 67--68 and
10	% 225). All verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks below were
11	% copied character-by-character from the rendered page images of the
12	% scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
13	
14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	
16	This chapter is the project-side build of the
17	\texttt{RR.2.H\(^1\)} sub-phase, committed in iter-188 of the strategy
18	revision: \emph{the first cohomology of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf
19	on a smooth proper curve vanishes}. Concretely, the chapter pins the
20	identity
21	\[
22	  \dim_{\bar k}\, H^1\bigl(C,\, \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
23	    (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)\bigr) \;=\; 0
24	\]
25	on a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\), where
26	\(P \in C\) is a closed point and the cohomology is computed through the
27	project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured \(\Ext\)-cohomology pipeline
28	\(\HModule \bar k\) (chapter~REF, sibling
29	\texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex}).
30	
31	\paragraph{Why a dedicated chapter.} Mathlib (snapshot
32	\texttt{b80f227}) does \emph{not} ship a flasque-cohomology vanishing
33	lemma at the \(\Ext\)-cohomology level used by
34	\(\HModule \bar k\), nor a Serre-duality theorem for curves. The
35	genus-\(0\) critical path nevertheless needs the skyscraper
36	\(H^1\)-vanishing twice: once inside the Hartshorne~IV.1.3
37	\(\chi\)-additivity assembly
38	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}, the parent lemma
39	\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_skyscraperSheaf}), and once inside the
40	\texttt{RR.3} chain that produces a non-constant section
41	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}, the downstream consumer
42	\texttt{h1\_vanishing\_genusZero}). The present chapter unifies the
43	substrate behind both consumers into a single short, self-contained
44	project-side build.
45	
46	\paragraph{Strategy of the chapter.} The argument is the classical
47	Hartshorne~III~\S2 one: an injective resolution of a flasque sheaf
48	yields a flasque quotient at each stage (since both the injective object
49	and the original sheaf are flasque), and the global-sections functor is
50	exact on a short exact sequence of flasque sheaves. Iterating, every
51	derived functor of \(\Gamma\) vanishes on a flasque input in positive
52	degree. The chapter then specialises to the closed-point skyscraper
53	sheaf, which is flasque by inspection (it is the pushforward, along the
54	inclusion of a one-point closed subspace, of the constant sheaf on an
55	irreducible base — and pushforwards of flasque sheaves are flasque).
56	
57	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is
58	an algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
59	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). The Lean signatures (planned location
60	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/H1Vanishing.lean}) carry the same
61	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API
62	(\(\mathtt{IsProper}\), \(\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\, 1\),
63	\(\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\), \(\mathtt{IsIntegral}\)). The
64	flasque-vanishing lemma of \S2 below is stated more generally for a
65	noetherian topological space (the natural generality of
66	Hartshorne~III.2.5); the curve specialisation is read off by instance
67	synthesis on the underlying space \(C.\mathtt{left.toTopCat}\).
68	
69	\section{Flasque sheaves and their cohomology vanishing}
70	\label{sec:H1Vanishing_flasque}
71	
72	\begin{definition}
73	
74	\leanok
75	[Flasque sheaf]
76	  \label{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
77	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque}
78	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.16 (definition of flasque
79	  % sheaf, opening paragraph) (read from
80	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 67); III.2,
81	  % paragraph preceding Lemma~2.4 (Hartshorne's own recall of the
82	  % definition) (PDF page 224).
83	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Flasque Sheaves. A sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on a
84	  % topological space \(X\) is \emph{flasque} if for every inclusion
85	  % \(V \subseteq U\) of open sets, the restriction map
86	  % \(\mathscr{F}(U) \to \mathscr{F}(V)\) is surjective."
87	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall (II, Ex. 1.16) that a sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on
88	  % a topological space \(X\) is \emph{flasque} if for every inclusion
89	  % of open sets \(V \subseteq U\), the restriction map
90	  % \(\mathscr{F}(U) \to \mathscr{F}(V)\) is surjective."
91	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.16 (definition); recalled
92	  III.2 preceding Lemma~2.4.}
93	  Let \((X, \mathcal O_X)\) be a ringed space and let \(\mathcal F\) be a
94	  sheaf of \(\mathcal O_X\)-modules on \(X\). We say that \(\mathcal F\)
95	  is \emph{flasque} if, for every inclusion of open sets
96	  \(V \subseteq U\) in \(X\), the restriction map
97	  \(\mathcal F(U) \to \mathcal F(V)\) is surjective.
98	
99	  In the project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured setup the predicate is
100	  applied to a sheaf
101	  \(\mathcal F : \mathrm{Sheaf}\;J\;(\Module \bar k)\), where \(J\) is the
102	  Grothendieck topology of opens on the underlying topological space of
103	  the curve \(C\). The surjectivity condition is read on the underlying
104	  presheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules; the project predicate
105	  \texttt{Scheme.IsFlasque} packages exactly this condition.
106	
107	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean predicate
108	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque} takes a sheaf
109	  \(\mathcal F : \mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
110	  (\Module \bar k)\) and asserts that for every pair \(V \le U\) of opens
111	  of \(X\), the restriction morphism
112	  \((\mathcal F.\mathtt{val.map}\;(\mathrm{leOp}\;V\;U)) :
113	  \mathcal F.\mathtt{val.obj}\;(\mathrm{op}\;U) \to
114	  \mathcal F.\mathtt{val.obj}\;(\mathrm{op}\;V)\) is surjective as a map of
115	  \(\bar k\)-modules.
116	\end{definition}
117	
118	\begin{lemma}
119	
120	\leanok
121	[Pushforward of a flasque sheaf is flasque]
122	  \label{lem:isFlasque_pushforward}
123	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.pushforward}
124	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
125	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.16(d) (read from
126	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 67).
127	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(f: X \to Y\) is a continuous map, and if
128	  % \(\mathscr{F}\) is a flasque sheaf on \(X\), then \(f_*\mathscr{F}\)
129	  % is a flasque sheaf on \(Y\)."
130	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.16(d).}
131	  Let \(f \colon X \to Y\) be a continuous map of topological spaces and
132	  let \(\mathcal F\) be a flasque sheaf on \(X\). Then the pushforward
133	  sheaf \(f_*\mathcal F\) is flasque on \(Y\).
134	\end{lemma}
135	
136	\begin{proof}
137	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
138	  \leanok
139	  For an inclusion of opens \(V \subseteq U\) in \(Y\), the restriction
140	  map of \(f_*\mathcal F\) is by definition the restriction map of
141	  \(\mathcal F\) on the open sets \(f^{-1}(V) \subseteq f^{-1}(U)\) of
142	  \(X\):
143	  \[
144	    (f_*\mathcal F)(U) \;=\; \mathcal F(f^{-1}(U))
145	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\mathcal F(f^{-1}(V) \subseteq f^{-1}(U))\;}\;
146	    \mathcal F(f^{-1}(V)) \;=\; (f_*\mathcal F)(V).
147	  \]
148	  Since \(\mathcal F\) is flasque on \(X\), the right-hand restriction is
149	  surjective, hence so is the left-hand one. Therefore \(f_*\mathcal F\)
150	  is flasque on \(Y\).
151	\end{proof}
152	
153	\begin{lemma}
154	
155	\leanok
156	[Constant sheaf on an irreducible space is flasque]
157	  \label{lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.constant_of_irreducible}
159	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
160	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.16(a) (read from
161	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 67).
162	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Show that a constant sheaf on an irreducible
163	  % topological space is flasque. See (I, \S1) for irreducible
164	  % topological spaces."
165	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.16(a).}
166	  Let \(X\) be an irreducible topological space and let \(A\) be a
167	  \(\bar k\)-module. Then the constant sheaf
168	  \((\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J\;\_)\;A\) on \(X\) is flasque.
169	\end{lemma}
170	
171	\begin{proof}
172	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
173	  On an irreducible topological space every non-empty open is connected
174	  and dense, so the constant presheaf
175	  \(U \mapsto A\) (with all non-empty restriction maps the identity)
176	  already satisfies the sheaf condition, and equals its sheafification.
177	  For every inclusion of non-empty opens \(V \subseteq U\), the
178	  restriction map is the identity on \(A\) — in particular surjective.
179	  For an inclusion involving the empty open, the target is the zero
180	  module, so surjectivity is immediate. Therefore the constant sheaf is
181	  flasque.
182	\end{proof}

184	\begin{lemma}
185	[Cokernel of a flasque-by-flasque short exact sequence is flasque]
186	  \label{lem:flasque_cokernel_short_exact}
187	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.cokernel_of_shortExact_flasque_flasque}
188	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
189	  % SOURCE: Project-bespoke substrate helper for the
190	  % Hartshorne~II.1~Exercise~1.16(c) / III.2.5 chain (iter-193 Lane H
191	  % prover-side construction). The classical reference is Hartshorne
192	  % II.1, Exercise~1.16(c); see the verbatim quote in the proof of
193	  % \cref{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque} below.
194	  \textit{Source: project-bespoke iter-193 substrate (Hartshorne~II.1,
195	  Exercise~1.16(c) packaged with the sections-surjectivity input from
196	  II.1, Exercise~1.16(b) as a hypothesis).}
197	  Let $X$ be a topological space and let
198	  \[
199	    0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0
200	  \]
201	  be a short exact sequence in
202	  $\mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
203	  (\Module \bar k)$. Suppose:
204	  \begin{enumerate}
205	    \item $\mathcal F_2$ is flasque (in the sense of
206	      \cref{def:isFlasque_sheaf}); and
207	    \item the section-level map
208	      $\mathcal F_2(U) \to \mathcal F_3(U)$ is surjective for every open
209	      $U \subseteq X$ (the Hartshorne~II.1, Exercise~1.16(b) input,
210	      packaged as a hypothesis rather than invoked internally — this
211	      keeps the lemma's axiom-set clean and lets the consumer site
212	      supply the surjectivity from its own substrate).
213	  \end{enumerate}
214	  Then the cokernel sheaf $\mathcal F_3$ is flasque.
215	
216	  Concretely, given $V \le U$ in $\Opens X$ and a section
217	  $t \in \mathcal F_3(V)$: lift $t$ to $\tilde t \in \mathcal F_2(V)$
218	  via hypothesis~(2) at $V$, extend $\tilde t$ to
219	  $\tilde T \in \mathcal F_2(U)$ via flasqueness of $\mathcal F_2$, and
220	  push $\tilde T$ through the map $\mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3$ at
221	  $U$. Naturality of the morphism on opens identifies the restriction
222	  of the resulting section in $\mathcal F_3(U)$ with the original $t$.
223	\end{lemma}

225	\begin{lemma}
226	[$\Ext^{n+1}$ vanishes from $\Ext^n$ vanishing on the rightmost term, with injective middle term]
227	  \label{lem:ext_succ_zero_of_injective_lower_zero}
228	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.ext_succ_eq_zero_of_injective_of_lower_zero}
229	  \uses{def:Scheme_HModule}
230	  % SOURCE: Project-bespoke substrate helper for the
231	  % Hartshorne~III.2.5 induction (iter-193 Lane H prover-side
232	  % construction). The underlying mathematical statement is the
233	  % classical degree-shifting argument that, for an injective middle
234	  % term in a short exact sequence, the connecting morphism in the
235	  % long exact $\Ext$ sequence is surjective above the injective
236	  % dimension. No external source is cited verbatim.
237	  \textit{Source: project-bespoke iter-193 substrate.}
238	  Let $\mathcal C$ be an abelian category equipped with a
239	  \texttt{HasExt} instance, let $X$ be an object of $\mathcal C$, and
240	  let
241	  \[
242	    0 \to S_1 \to S_2 \to S_3 \to 0
243	  \]
244	  be a short exact sequence in $\mathcal C$ whose middle term $S_2$ is
245	  injective. Let $n_0 \ge 1$ be a natural number and assume that
246	  $\Ext^{n_0}(X, S_3) = 0$ (i.e.\ every element of $\Ext^{n_0}(X, S_3)$
247	  vanishes). Then $\Ext^{n_0 + 1}(X, S_1) = 0$.
248	
249	  The proof is the classical degree-shift. Since $S_2$ is injective and
250	  $n_0 + 1 \ge 2$, the injective-dimension subsingleton instance forces
251	  $\Ext^{n_0 + 1}(X, S_2)$ to be subsingleton; combined with exactness
252	  of the covariant $\Ext$ long exact sequence at $\Ext^{n_0 + 1}(X,
253	  S_1)$, every $x_1 \in \Ext^{n_0 + 1}(X, S_1)$ is in the image of the
254	  connecting morphism $\delta \colon \Ext^{n_0}(X, S_3) \to
255	  \Ext^{n_0 + 1}(X, S_1)$. The hypothesis
256	  $\Ext^{n_0}(X, S_3) = 0$ then gives $x_1 = 0$. The lemma is consumed
257	  in the $i \ge 2$ inductive case of \cref{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque}.
258	\end{lemma}

260	\begin{theorem}
261	
262	\leanok
263	[Flasque sheaves have vanishing higher cohomology]
264	  \label{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque}
265	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule_flasque_eq_zero}
266	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
267	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.2, Proposition~2.5 (read from
268	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 225).
269	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 2.5. \emph{If \(\mathscr{F}\) is a
270	  % flasque sheaf on a topological space \(X\), then
271	  % \(H^i(X, \mathscr{F}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\).}"
272	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.2, Proposition~2.5, p.~208.}
273	  Let \(X\) be a topological space and let \(\mathcal F\) be a flasque
274	  sheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules on \(X\), viewed as an object of
275	  \(\mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
276	  (\Module \bar k)\). Then, for every \(i \ge 1\),
277	  \[
278	    \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;i \;=\; 0.
279	  \]
280	  In particular, \(\dim_{\bar k} \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 = 0\).
281	\end{theorem}
282	
283	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. Embed \(\mathscr{F}\) in an injective
284	% object \(\mathscr{I}\) of \(\mathfrak{Ab}(X)\) and let \(\mathscr{G}\)
285	% be the quotient:
286	% \(0 \to \mathscr{F} \to \mathscr{I} \to \mathscr{G} \to 0\).
287	% Then \(\mathscr{F}\) is flasque by hypothesis, \(\mathscr{I}\) is
288	% flasque by (2.4), and so \(\mathscr{G}\) is flasque by (II, Ex. 1.16c).
289	% Now since \(\mathscr{F}\) is flasque, we have an exact sequence (II,
290	% Ex. 1.16b)
291	% \(0 \to \Gamma(X, \mathscr{F}) \to \Gamma(X, \mathscr{I}) \to
292	% \Gamma(X, \mathscr{G}) \to 0\).
293	% On the other hand, since \(\mathscr{I}\) is injective, we have
294	% \(H^i(X, \mathscr{I}) = 0\) for \(i > 0\) (1.1Ae). Thus from the long
295	% exact sequence of cohomology, we get \(H^1(X, \mathscr{F}) = 0\) and
296	% \(H^i(X, \mathscr{F}) \cong H^{i-1}(X, \mathscr{G})\) for each
297	% \(i \geq 2\). But \(\mathscr{G}\) is also flasque, so by induction on
298	% \(i\) we get the result."
299	\begin{proof}
300	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
301	  \leanok
302	  We follow Hartshorne's induction. Embed \(\mathcal F\) into an
303	  injective object \(\mathcal I\) of the abelian category
304	  \(\mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
305	  (\Module \bar k)\) — such an injective exists by the
306	  enough-injectives instance for sheaves of \(\bar k\)-modules on opens
307	  (\cref{chap:Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK}). Form the quotient
308	  short exact sequence
309	  \[
310	    0 \to \mathcal F \to \mathcal I \to \mathcal G \to 0
311	  \]
312	  in the sheaf category.
313	
314	  \emph{Step 1: the quotient \(\mathcal G\) is flasque.} The hypothesis
315	  gives that \(\mathcal F\) is flasque. The injective object
316	  \(\mathcal I\) is flasque by the Hartshorne~III~Lemma~2.4 input:
317	  any injective \(\mathcal O_X\)-module is flasque, where the
318	  \(\mathcal O_X\) here is the constant sheaf of rings \(\bar k\) on
319	  \(X\), so the sheaf category over the constant sheaf of rings is the
320	  category of sheaves of \(\bar k\)-modules and the lemma applies. From
321	  the two flasqueness inputs the quotient \(\mathcal G\) inherits
322	  flasqueness by Hartshorne~II~Exercise~1.16(c): \emph{if
323	  \(0 \to \mathcal F' \to \mathcal F \to \mathcal F'' \to 0\) is an
324	  exact sequence and \(\mathcal F', \mathcal F\) are flasque, then
325	  \(\mathcal F''\) is flasque}.
326	
327	  \emph{Step 2: the global-sections functor is exact on the chosen
328	  short exact sequence.} Since the leftmost term \(\mathcal F\) is
329	  flasque, the long exact sequence of cohomology starts with a short
330	  exact sequence on global sections
331	  (Hartshorne~II~Exercise~1.16(b)):
332	  \[
333	    0 \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal F) \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal I) \to
334	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal G) \to 0.
335	  \]
336	
337	  \emph{Step 3: the long exact sequence gives the vanishing.}
338	  The object \(\mathcal I\) is injective in the abelian sheaf category,
339	  hence its right-derived global-sections cohomology vanishes in
340	  positive degree:
341	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal I\;i = 0\) for all \(i \ge 1\). The long
342	  exact sequence of the short exact sequence above, applied to the
343	  derived functor \(\HModule\;\bar k\;{-}\;{\bullet}\) of \(\Gamma\),
344	  reads in degrees \(0\) and \(1\) as
345	  \[
346	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal I) \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal G)
347	    \to \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1
348	    \to \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal I\;1 = 0.
349	  \]
350	  The leftmost map is surjective by Step~2, so the connecting morphism
351	  to \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1\) is the zero map, and the kernel
352	  of \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 \to 0\) is all of
353	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1\); hence
354	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 = 0\).
355	
356	  \emph{Step 4: higher degrees by induction.} For \(i \ge 2\), the long
357	  exact sequence yields
358	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;i \cong \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal G\;
359	  (i - 1)\); the inductive hypothesis on the flasque quotient
360	  \(\mathcal G\) gives the right-hand side is zero. This closes the
361	  vanishing in all positive degrees. The chapter consumes only the
362	  degree-\(1\) instance, but the lemma is stated and proved in full
363	  generality so it can support later sub-builds (e.g.\ Grothendieck
364	  vanishing on a one-dimensional scheme).
365	
366	  % NOTE: the proof structure mirrors Hartshorne~III~Proposition~2.5
367	  % verbatim. The Lean closure is gated on three substrate inputs:
368	  % (a) the enough-injectives instance for `Sheaf J (ModuleCat kbar)`
369	  % already provided by chapter
370	  % `Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK.tex`; (b) the project-side
371	  % flasque-quotient lemma (Hartshorne~II~Ex.~1.16(c)) restated for
372	  % `ModuleCat kbar`-valued sheaves; (c) the long-exact-sequence
373	  % carrier for `Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence` at the `HModule kbar`
374	  % level (the same LES carrier consumed by
375	  % `eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add` in
376	  % `RiemannRoch_RRFormula.tex`). All three are project-side ~50-100
377	  % LOC apiece.
378	\end{proof}
